perturbation.original_text: 1. Definition. “Confidential Information” shall, mean all intellectual property, business plans, financial records and strategies, marketing plans, contacts, trade secrets, information, materials, documentation and software, as well as copies of all such materials made thereof as authorized by this Agreement, including written, oral, or verbal disclosures. “Confidential Information” shall not include: a. Information that was in the public domain, in its entirety in a unified form, at the time of disclosure to Receiving Party by Vapotherm; b. Information known to Receiving Party prior to its disclosure by Vapotherm, such prior knowledge to be demonstrated by Receiving Party’s records prepared before the date the Confidential Information is disclosed by Vapotherm; c. Information that becomes part of the public domain after the date of disclosure by Vapotherm through no fault of Receiving Party; d. Information that is disclosed by a third party to Receiving Party after the date of disclosure by Vapotherm, where the third party did not require Receiving Party to hold such information in confidence and did not acquire such information directly or indirectly from Vapotherm.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. Definition. “Confidential Information” shall mean all intellectual property, business plans, financial records and strategies, marketing plans, contacts, trade secrets, information, materials, documentation and software, as well as copies of all such materials made thereof as authorized by this Agreement, including written, oral, or verbal disclosures. Notwithstanding the foregoing, Confidential Information shall only include information explicitly marked as 'Confidential' by Vapotherm at the time of disclosure. “Confidential Information” shall not include: a. Information that was in the public domain, in its entirety in a unified form, at the time of disclosure to Receiving Party by Vapotherm; b. Information known to Receiving Party prior to its disclosure by Vapotherm, such prior knowledge to be demonstrated by Receiving Party’s records prepared before the date the Confidential Information is disclosed by Vapotherm; c. Information that becomes part of the public domain after the date of disclosure by Vapotherm through no fault of Receiving Party; d. Information that is disclosed by a third party to Receiving Party after the date of disclosure by Vapotherm, where the third party did not require Receiving Party to hold such information in confidence and did not acquire such information directly or indirectly from Vapotherm.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction. Initially, 'Confidential Information' broadly encompasses various documents and information. The added clause stating that 'Confidential Information shall only include information explicitly marked as 'Confidential' by Vapotherm at the time of disclosure' creates a conflict. Other sections of the agreement (e.g., Section 3) rely on the initial broad definition to enforce non-disclosure, but with this modification, those sections become ambiguous if the information was not explicitly marked as confidential.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. Nondisclosure. The Receiving Party agrees that, in consideration of access to the Confidential Information, it shall hold such Confidential Information in strict confidence and shall take all measures necessary to prevent the Confidential Information from falling into the public domain or into the possession of the persons not bound to this Agreement. The Receiving Party further agrees: a. To use Confidential Information for the Permitted Purpose; b. Not to disclose Confidential Information to any other entity, except as expressly authorized in writing by Vapotherm; c. Not to use Confidential Information in such a way as to hinder, interfere with, or in any way circumvent Vapotherm’s interest in the Technology, or consulting or business relations between and among Vapotherm and third parties; d. Not to copy Confidential Information in whole or in part, without the express written permission of Vapotherm; e. To return Confidential Information, including all copies and records thereof, to Vapotherm, at the Receiving Party’s expense, within five (5) business days after: (i) receipt of a written request from Vapotherm; (ii) a decision by Receiving Party not to enter into any consulting relationship with Vapotherm; or, (iii) within thirty (30) days following the termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a requirement that 'Confidential Information shall only include information explicitly marked as 'Confidential' by Vapotherm at the time of disclosure.' This directly contradicts Section 3, which obligates the Receiving Party to protect *all* Confidential Information as broadly defined in the original text (intellectual property, business plans, etc.). If Vapotherm fails to mark certain information falling under the original definition as 'Confidential,' the Receiving Party is no longer obligated to protect it under the modified agreement, creating a strong and irreconcilable conflict.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Nondisclosure. The Receiving Party agrees that, in consideration of access to the Confidential Information, it shall hold such Confidential Information in strict confidence and shall take all measures necessary to prevent the Confidential Information from falling into the public domain or into the possession of the persons not bound to this Agreement. The Receiving Party further agrees: a. To use Confidential Information for the Permitted Purpose; b. Not to disclose Confidential Information to any other entity, except as expressly authorized in writing by Vapotherm; c. Not to use Confidential Information in such a way as to hinder, interfere with, or in any way circumvent Vapotherm’s interest in the Technology, or consulting or business relations between and among Vapotherm and third parties; d. Not to copy Confidential Information in whole or in part, without the express written permission of Vapotherm; e. To return Confidential Information, including all copies and records thereof, to Vapotherm, at the Receiving Party’s expense, within five (5) business days after: (i) receipt of a written request from Vapotherm; (ii) a decision by Receiving Party not to enter into any consulting relationship with Vapotherm; or, (iii) within thirty (30) days following the termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Nondisclosure. The Receiving Party agrees that, in consideration of access to the Confidential Information, it shall use commercially reasonable efforts to hold such Confidential Information in confidence and shall take measures necessary to prevent the Confidential Information from falling into the public domain or into the possession of the persons not bound to this Agreement. The Receiving Party further agrees: a. To use Confidential Information for the Permitted Purpose; b. Not to disclose Confidential Information to any other entity, except as expressly authorized in writing by Vapotherm, unless compelled by law; c. Not to use Confidential Information in such a way as to hinder, interfere with, or in any way circumvent Vapotherm’s interest in the Technology, or consulting or business relations between and among Vapotherm and third parties; d. Not to copy Confidential Information in whole or in part, without the express written permission of Vapotherm; e. To return Confidential Information, including all copies and records thereof, to Vapotherm, at the Receiving Party’s expense, within five (5) business days after: (i) receipt of a written request from Vapotherm; (ii) a decision by Receiving Party not to enter into any consulting relationship with Vapotherm; or, (iii) within thirty (30) days following the termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall hold such Confidential Information in strict confidence' to 'shall use commercially reasonable efforts to hold such Confidential Information in confidence' and adding 'unless compelled by law', a contradiction is introduced. Initially, the obligation to hold information in confidence was strict and absolute. Now, the standard is relaxed to 'commercially reasonable efforts,' and an exception is carved out for disclosures compelled by law. This contradicts the intent of Section 5 on 'Injunctive Relief' where Vapotherm is entitled to 'irreparable harm' if breached. The changes on the nondisclosure section makes it now harder to get Injunctive Relief.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5. Injunctive Relief. Because of the unique nature of the Confidential information, the Receiving Party understands and agrees that Vapotherm will suffer irreparable harm in the event that any party hereto fails to comply with any of the terms of the Agreement, and monetary damages may be inadequate to compensate such breach. Accordingly, the Receiving Party agrees that Vapotherm will, in addition to any other remedies available to it at law or in equity, be entitled to injunctive relief to enforce the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text stipulated that the Receiving Party 'shall hold such Confidential Information in strict confidence,' implying an absolute obligation. The changed text modifies this to 'shall use commercially reasonable efforts to hold such Confidential Information in confidence' and adds an exception, 'unless compelled by law.' This introduces a weaker, more flexible standard of care. The 'Injunctive Relief' clause (Section 5) states that Vapotherm will suffer 'irreparable harm' if the agreement is breached and is entitled to injunctive relief. The change from 'strict confidence' to 'commercially reasonable efforts,' along with the added 'unless compelled by law' clause, directly contradicts the Injunctive Relief clause. The weaker standard of care and the exception make it harder for Vapotherm to prove 'irreparable harm' and obtain injunctive relief because the Receiving Party can argue they used 'commercially reasonable efforts' or were 'compelled by law' to disclose the information. Therefore, a strong in-text contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: 7. Inventions. Inventions means all discoveries, improvements, modifications and enhancements relating to the Technology that are conceived, developed or reduced to practice by Receiving Party during die Term, either solely or jointly with others: (i) during the course of or as a result of performing the Permitted Purpose, or (ii) that are -2- related to or attributable to Vapotherm’s products, or later modifications thereof, whether patentable or not, and all Intellectual Property rights therein. All Inventions will be immediately reported to Vapotherm and shall be the exclusive property of Vapotherm. The Receiving Party will perform, at Vapotherm’s request and expense, any act which it can reasonably perform that Vapotherm deems necessary to vest title to such inventions and ideas in Vapotherm and to execute any and all patent applications in connection therewith.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. Inventions. Inventions means all discoveries, improvements, modifications and enhancements relating to the Technology that are conceived, developed or reduced to practice by Receiving Party during die Term, either solely or jointly with others: (i) during the course of or as a result of performing the Permitted Purpose, or (ii) that are -2- related to or attributable to Vapotherm’s products, or later modifications thereof, whether patentable or not, and all Intellectual Property rights therein. Inventions made using Receiving Party's own time and resources will be jointly owned by both parties. All other Inventions will be immediately reported to Vapotherm and shall be the exclusive property of Vapotherm. The Receiving Party will perform, at Vapotherm’s request and expense, any act which it can reasonably perform that Vapotherm deems necessary to vest title to such inventions and ideas in Vapotherm and to execute any and all patent applications in connection therewith.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a contradiction regarding the ownership of inventions. Initially, all inventions related to the Technology were the exclusive property of Vapotherm. The added sentence, 'Inventions made using Receiving Party's own time and resources will be jointly owned by both parties,' creates a scenario where some inventions are exclusively Vapotherm's and others are jointly owned, depending on the resources used. This contradicts the blanket statement that 'All Inventions will be immediately reported to Vapotherm and shall be the exclusive property of Vapotherm,' creating confusion and potential disputes over intellectual property ownership.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 7. Inventions. Inventions means all discoveries, improvements, modifications and enhancements relating to the Technology that are conceived, developed or reduced to practice by Receiving Party during die Term, either solely or jointly with others: (i) during the course of or as a result of performing the Permitted Purpose, or (ii) that are -2- related to or attributable to Vapotherm’s products, or later modifications thereof, whether patentable or not, and all Intellectual Property rights therein. Inventions made using Receiving Party's own time and resources will be jointly owned by both parties. All other Inventions will be immediately reported to Vapotherm and shall be the exclusive property of Vapotherm. The Receiving Party will perform, at Vapotherm’s request and expense, any act which it can reasonably perform that Vapotherm deems necessary to vest title to such inventions and ideas in Vapotherm and to execute any and all patent applications in connection therewith.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction by stating that 'Inventions made using Receiving Party's own time and resources will be jointly owned by both parties.' This directly conflicts with the initial statement in the original text, which remains in the changed text, that 'All Inventions will be immediately reported to Vapotherm and shall be the exclusive property of Vapotherm.' This creates a clear inconsistency regarding the ownership of inventions, leading to potential disputes. The contradicted text is the same section as the changed text, yet it now contains conflicting information about ownership.

perturbation.original_text: 11. Survival. The restrictions and obligations of this Agreement shall continue and shall survive the termination of the Purpose of the disclosure for a period of five (5) years.
perturbation.changed_text: 11. Survival. The restrictions and obligations of this Agreement shall continue and shall survive the termination of the Purpose of the disclosure for a period of five (5) years, except for obligations regarding trade secrets, which shall survive indefinitely.
perturbation.explanation: The original agreement states that all restrictions and obligations survive for five years after termination. The modified version adds an exception: 'except for obligations regarding trade secrets, which shall survive indefinitely.' This change creates a contradiction. While the general survival clause limits the duration to five years, the added clause suggests that obligations related to trade secrets last forever. This is a contradiction. For example, an information could be considered confidential but also a trade secret, so it is unclear the duration of the confidentiallity. This uncertainty impacts enforceability, especially regarding the definition of Confidential Information in Section 1 and the obligations regarding such in Section 3.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1. Definition. “Confidential Information” shall mean all intellectual property, business plans, financial records and strategies, marketing plans, contacts, trade secrets, information, materials, documentation and software, as well as copies of all such materials made thereof as authorized by this Agreement, including written, oral, or verbal disclosures. Notwithstanding the foregoing, Confidential Information shall only include information explicitly marked as 'Confidential' by Vapotherm at the time of disclosure. “Confidential Information” shall not include: a. Information that was in the public domain, in its entirety in a unified form, at the time of disclosure to Receiving Party by Vapotherm; b. Information known to Receiving Party prior to its disclosure by Vapotherm, such prior knowledge to be demonstrated by Receiving Party’s records prepared before the date the Confidential Information is disclosed by Vapotherm; c. Information that becomes part of the public domain after the date of disclosure by Vapotherm through no fault of Receiving Party; d. Information that is disclosed by a third party to Receiving Party after the date of disclosure by Vapotherm, where the third party did not require Receiving Party to hold such information in confidence and did not acquire such information directly or indirectly from Vapotherm.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an exception to the survival clause that contradicts the definition of 'Confidential Information'. The original text states all restrictions and obligations survive for five years. The changed text adds an exception for trade secrets, which survive indefinitely. Section 1 defines 'Confidential Information' to include 'trade secrets' but also allows for information to only be considered confidential if 'explicitly marked as Confidential'. Because of this definition, some confidential information will expire after 5 years, some might survive indefinitely, creating a contradiction. This impacts the definition in Section 1 and the obligations related to it, creating a clear and direct contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 12. Interpretation; Venue. This Agreement shall be interpreted and enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, without reference to its choice of law provisions. The parties agree that any legal action or proceedings brought by or against them with respect to this Agreement shall be brought in the state or federal courts located in Delaware, USA and, by execution and delivery hereof, the parties hereby irrevocably submit to each such jurisdiction and hereby irrevocably waive any and all objections which they may have with respect to venue in any of the above courts.
perturbation.changed_text: 12. Interpretation; Venue. This Agreement shall be interpreted and enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, without reference to its choice of law provisions, except for matters concerning intellectual property, which shall be governed by the laws of the United States. The parties agree that any legal action or proceedings brought by or against them with respect to this Agreement shall be brought in the state or federal courts located in Delaware, USA and, by execution and delivery hereof, the parties hereby irrevocably submit to each such jurisdiction and hereby irrevocably waive any and all objections which they may have with respect to venue in any of the above courts.
perturbation.explanation: This modification creates an in-text contradiction by specifying that the laws of the State of Delaware govern the agreement's interpretation and enforcement, but then adding 'except for matters concerning intellectual property, which shall be governed by the laws of the United States.' Section 7 governs Inventions, introducing a conflict as it's unclear whether Delaware or US law applies to inventions-related disputes. The definition and treatment of intellectual property rights could differ significantly under Delaware and US law, leading to interpretative problems and increasing the likelihood of legal disputes.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 7. Inventions. Inventions means all discoveries, improvements, modifications and enhancements relating to the Technology that are conceived, developed or reduced to practice by Receiving Party during die Term, either solely or jointly with others: (i) during the course of or as a result of performing the Permitted Purpose, or (ii) that are -2- related to or attributable to Vapotherm’s products, or later modifications thereof, whether patentable or not, and all Intellectual Property rights therein. Inventions made using Receiving Party's own time and resources will be jointly owned by both parties. All other Inventions will be immediately reported to Vapotherm and shall be the exclusive property of Vapotherm. The Receiving Party will perform, at Vapotherm’s request and expense, any act which it can reasonably perform that Vapotherm deems necessary to vest title to such inventions and ideas in Vapotherm and to execute any and all patent applications in connection therewith.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds an exception to the governing law provision, stating that matters concerning intellectual property shall be governed by the laws of the United States, while the original text stated that the agreement shall be interpreted and enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware. This creates a direct conflict with Section 7, which concerns 'Inventions' and related Intellectual Property rights. It becomes unclear whether Delaware or US law applies to Inventions-related disputes, leading to a strong contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 17. Modification. No addition to, deletion from, or modification of the provisions of this Agreement shall be permitted or shall be binding upon a party hereto unless made in writing and signed by each party.
perturbation.changed_text: 17. Modification. No addition to, deletion from, or modification of the provisions of this Agreement shall be permitted or shall be binding upon a party hereto unless made in writing and signed by each party. Notwithstanding the foregoing, modifications may be made electronically via email if both parties acknowledge receipt.
perturbation.explanation: The original agreement states that all modifications must be in writing and signed by both parties. This change adds a conflicting clause: 'Notwithstanding the foregoing, modifications may be made electronically via email if both parties acknowledge receipt.' This creates ambiguity. If the parties agree to a change via email (and acknowledge receipt), is that modification valid despite not being 'in writing and signed by each party'? Section 3(b) stated that no disclousure can be made 'except as expressly authorized in writing by Vapotherm' this creates a new confusion on how written authorization can be given.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. Nondisclosure. The Receiving Party agrees that, in consideration of access to the Confidential Information, it shall use commercially reasonable efforts to hold such Confidential Information in confidence and shall take measures necessary to prevent the Confidential Information from falling into the public domain or into the possession of the persons not bound to this Agreement. The Receiving Party further agrees: a. To use Confidential Information for the Permitted Purpose; b. Not to disclose Confidential Information to any other entity, except as expressly authorized in writing by Vapotherm, unless compelled by law; c. Not to use Confidential Information in such a way as to hinder, interfere with, or in any way circumvent Vapotherm’s interest in the Technology, or consulting or business relations between and among Vapotherm and third parties; d. Not to copy Confidential Information in whole or in part, without the express written permission of Vapotherm; e. To return Confidential Information, including all copies and records thereof, to Vapotherm, at the Receiving Party’s expense, within five (5) business days after: (i) receipt of a written request from Vapotherm; (ii) a decision by Receiving Party not to enter into any consulting relationship with Vapotherm; or, (iii) within thirty (30) days following the termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original agreement (Section 17) mandates written and signed modifications. The changed text introduces an exception: modifications via email with acknowledgment. This contradicts Section 3(b), which requires express written authorization from Vapotherm for any disclosure. The email acknowledgment, while fulfilling Section 17's new clause, might not meet the 'expressly authorized in writing' requirement of Section 3(b), causing a strong in-text contradiction. A reasonable reader could immediately recognize these sections as potentially incompatible, leading to uncertainty regarding which modification method is valid and whether email acknowledgment fulfills the written authorization requirement.
